candidate.title: HyperThings I
candidate.description: This is a fantastic set of externals by HyperThings. It is the first of many collections of externals that will be released here in America Online's Mac HyperCard forum. Some of the most fun and useful externals are described below: ArcProgress: Will display color pies and arcs on the screen, allowing you to make *fast* pie-type progress screens. DoAlert 2.0: Will display color dialogs with multiple buttons, color picts and icons, even on-the-fly text, all in one simple line of code! GetPixCol: Get the color of a pixel *anywhere* on the screen. GetRGBColor: Will let you use Apple's standard color picker dialog to get a precise RGB value! MoveRect: Smoothly scroll any rect in the hypercard window! RefreshRect: Update (also known as 'refresh') any rect in the hypercard window! SetCCurs: Now you can set the cursor to a color crsr resource! SimplePop: Lets you make on-the-fly popup menus with ICONs, CICN's, multiple styles, commmand chars, checkmarks, disabled items, and divider lines! This collection has 15 different externals, and the stack is unlocked so you can examine all of it's scripts. It's a great collection of shareware externals that any HyperCard user will find irresistible! Please note: There has been a change of address for HyperThings. If you would like the new address, please send an email to UFOfreak5@aol.com and request the new address. Thank you. STAFF NOTE: Interesting set. Checked for Viruses with Disinfectant & Merry Xmas Vaccine.

candidate.title: PlayMovie 4.0 XCMD (Director ...
candidate.description: An external allowing you to play a Director 4.0 movie from within HyperCard. This external is not officially supported by MacroMedia. I was told by MM that an engineer had donated his time to write the command. The limited documentation says to refer to the Player 3.0 stack that shipped with Director 3.0 as the documentation for this stack. But there are major differences between the way this XCMD works now and the way it did in the past. The old version supported the movieShowMenu and movieHideMenu parameters which allowed scripters to control HC's menubar when a movie played. The new version ALWAYS hides the menubar, and it will remain hidden until the command playMovie movieClose completely dumps the player from RAM. This can be VERY distracting if you have designed an 'application' with your own menus!

candidate.title: Sun and Moon 3.0 Demo.sit
candidate.description: Welcome to the standalone Sun and Moon 3.0 Demo, which develops association and visual discrimination skills. Children find two objects that go together in the first activity. They point to the picture of the bird and the nest, for instance, rather than the car or the planet. In an alternate activity, students see a scene in which there are objects that don't belong. If they pick out all of the objects, they receive a reward! Sun and Moon encourages critical thinking, while appealing to children with its colorful graphics and sound. This demo version is fully functional, with sounds and animation, but only contains a sampling of the association and discrimination activities. Look for the other two titles in the Early Learning Series here online: Alphabet Zoo and Number Aquarium.

candidate.title: Animation Tutorial v1.1
candidate.description: Version 1.1 of the Animation stack includes the following changes: (1) a new Title card, created with Aldus Type Twister (2) changes to the Drag animation to show user interface (3) changes to the Drawn Animation to show more realistic motion (4) changes to the butterfly routine to include art work (5) updating of the affected scripts. This is a menu driven tutorial stack that demystifies the writing of scripts to do animation. Annotated animation scripts have been copied into a scrolling field on a glossary card. The glossary itself is in another scrolling field on the same card. Each entry in the glossary consists of the term, in boldface type, followed by its explanation. Users can repeatedly go from the animation to the glossary by using the menu that is provided. This stack is freeware. Any feedback that you take the time to send, will be appreciated. If you decide to use it, please fill out and mail the Registration Form that is provided or register via email to: ArthurD12@AOL.com or DieliAV@eWorld.com.

candidate.title: John C. Dvorak Insult Stack.sit
candidate.description: ----------------- These sounds are from the audio part of the free Apple-MacUser CD-ROM Sampler. candidate.title: Version Control 2.1.SIT
candidate.description: Two external commands are attached to this stack. They give the stack developer a way to attach version numbers and comments to a stack in a 'vers' resource (like the resource that is attached to the HyperCard application). A Macintosh 'vers' resource stores 4 items: the version number in BCD form, the version number in ascii (text), a version comment, and a number indicating the country the file is localized for. If you "Get Info" on a file in Finder 6.1 or later, the version comment is the text that is displayed. The other data is available to any Mac program that opens the resource. You can use the ReadVersion XFCN to read the version number, the version comment, and the country code. It returns them as comma-separated items in a text string. (The comment will be enclosed in quotes.) You need to be in the stack in order to read its 'vers' data, so go there first if you're not. ReadVersion will return an error message if anything goes wrong. To change your stack's 'vers' resource, use the WriteVersion XCMD. It takes 2 or 3 parameters: the version number, the version comment and, optionally, the country code. It will update the 'vers' resource of the current stack, (or create one if none existed). Check 'the result' for error messages after calling WriteVersion.
candidate.modernAnalog: application build metadata editor/reader for version strings, release notes, and locale tags
candidate.coreUserJob: set and retrieve version metadata on a software artifact so builds can be identified and described consistently
candidate.whyRelevant: The card describes a durable release-management job: writing and reading structured version metadata, comments, and localization codes attached to an artifact. That maps cleanly to modern build pipelines, package metadata, app manifest versioning, and release labeling workflows.
candidate.likelyStructure: read/write utility over a current artifact with fields for version number, human-readable comment, optional locale/country code, and explicit error reporting
candidate.searchQuerySeed: build artifact version metadata editor release notes locale tag read write workflow

candidate.title: StartupMooV 2.0!
candidate.description: StartupMooV 2.0 by Ben Clark Have you ever wanted to see a QuickTime(tm) movie when you start up your computer? I'm sure a lot of you have. That is why I have renewed my contract on this stack -- and made a new version. This stack will take a QuickTime(tm) movie of your choice and install it into the System Folder under the name of . This way, you can edit it and shorten it before you make it your startup movie. (When played as a startup movie, the movies play at full volume and length with no possible aborting -- unless you start up without QuickTime installed). I hope you enjoy the new version of StartupMooV. I welcome suggestions and comments sent to either my account or my account. ----*----*----*---- StartupMooV 2.0 is (c)1994-1995 Script Wizards. It gives an explanation of numerology and calculates your name and birth numbers. The stack includes instructions for ordering the extended version which also picks your lucky Lotto numbers, calculates your luckiest days, and tells which careers you're most suited for. The file was compressed with Stuffit 1.5.1.

judge.reasoning: The discard decision is correct. This is a shareware demo for novelty numerology entertainment - calculating 'personal numbers' from names and birth dates with fortune-style interpretations. The underlying job is pure amusement content, not a reusable workflow, operational utility, or sovereign/local-first pattern. The candidate's rationale accurately identifies this as 'novelty divination rather than a broadly reusable operational workflow.' The structure (input form → computed novelty results → upsell) is a trivial calculator pattern with no module merit. No surviving bounded workflow exists beyond generic entertainment content that modern users would find in countless free online fortune calculators. Historical curiosity about 1990s shareware packaging does not rescue this. Discard is appropriate.

outcome.modernAnalog: lightweight entertainment quiz or fortune-style calculator
outcome.coreUserJob: generate personalized novelty interpretations from a user's name and birth date for amusement
outcome.whyRelevant: Correctly identified as novelty entertainment without a durable module pattern or operational workflow. The underlying job is amusement content, not a reusable sovereign/local-first utility.
outcome.likelyStructure: intro/explanation screen, user input form for name and birth date, computed result views for numbers and interpretations, upsell to extended outputs
outcome.searchQuerySeed: personalized quiz result generator name birth date interpretation workflow

metaVerification.reasoning: The primary judge correctly identifies this as a novelty entertainment artifact with no durable module pattern. The reasoning stays grounded in the raw card text: it describes a shareware demo for numerology 'fortune telling' (name/birth number calculations, lotto predictions, career guesses). This is a clear example of 'content-only stack without reusable workflow' that the gate should reject. The discard decision is trustworthy.

candidate.title: +Quest For California demo
candidate.description: Staff Pick -- very nicely done Type: freeware demo This is a free demo of one of the series of 'Quest For California', which can be ordered from Mines Road Books. Quest For California is a fun and unique quiz game of California history. Each series contains five or more major historic events which are shown in colorful animations with brief captions. After viewing the animated event, you are asked to give the name of the person, place, event or date. The object is to get a high score and learn California history, the fun way. Quest For California offers a visual method of helping students remember historic events. This demo contains two of the major historic events from the Mexican Period. The series is designed for ages 9 and up.
candidate.modernAnalog: interactive history learning quiz module
candidate.coreUserJob: help learners study and recall historical events through short animated lessons followed by quiz prompts
candidate.whyRelevant: The card maps cleanly to a durable educational pattern: present a compact multimedia lesson, then test recall with scored questions. That lesson-plus-quiz structure is still directly reusable in modern learning, museum, and classroom content modules.
candidate.likelyStructure: topic intro, animated event scenes with captions, prompt asking for person/place/event/date, answer evaluation, score tracking, progression across multiple events
candidate.searchQuerySeed: interactive history lesson quiz module animated event recall scoring

candidate.title: 1st. Law of Thermodynamics
candidate.description: Keywords : Freshman Chemistry Chemical Thermodynamics Chemistry lessons This stack is an advertisement for a set of lessons on the first law of thermodynamics (as it is seen from the chemical point of view). This is the first set of lessons in the CheMentor series of software products. The set consists of six lessons and six tests which can be used on individual machines or on a networked system of Macs. They have been tested with Freshman students and received enthusiastic reviews. The basic ideas of system, surroundings, energy transfers (heat and work), internal energy, measurement of internal energy change (or E, if you like), enthalpy, measurement of enthalpy change, relation between enthalpy change and internal energy change, thermochemical equations and Hess's law are all covered. Product will include full documentation. See also the demos 'Chemistry Tutoring' (which outlines the CheMentor series in more detail) and 'The Chemistry Toolkit' - also the Shareware product 'The Molar Mass Calculator' - all of which are available from AOL (use Command-5 or the GoTo menu (File Search)).

candidate.title: Skytalks - Deconstructing DeFeNeStRaTe.C - Slides
candidate.description: In 2012 a hacker in Cambodia gained unauthorized access to a mainframe in Sweden. This breach has been talked about in depth by this speaker and many others. One little known aspect of the breach was the release of a program on pastebin called 'DeFeNeStRaTe.C'. This program uses a buffer overflow attack against an APF authorized Unix (USS) program: IOELMD10. In this session we will walk through exactly what the attack does, how it worked, a demo against an APF authorized program in Unix on z/OS and how you can detect and protect APF authorized Unix programs. If you wish to prepare the source code is available here: https://github.com/mainframed/logica/blob/master/DeFeNeStRaTe.C

candidate.title: The Meaning of Life 2.0
candidate.description: What is the meaning of life? Mankind has searched for the answer to that one for years. Many of us make it our lifelong occupation. Some of us are lucky enough to find some degree of satisfaction in the answers we get. Others of us never come close. In any event, pursuit of the answer to this question can be very rewarding. A good foundation in the concepts presented here can contribute to your success, no matter what endeavors you attempt. What is 'The Meaning of Life?' In this case it is a HyperCard Stack which explores the key philosophical concepts which codify the thoughts of some of the greatest thinkers in history. The stack uses the user-friendly HyperCard interface to explore key concepts, the history of philosophy, key documents, biographies, and quotes from the greats on the meaning of life and human nature.

candidate.title: Color Pictures
candidate.description: An XCMD to show PICT files or resources in HyperCard's window. As well as a bonus set of scripts to make pseudo picture objects. Way back in 1989 I had a color macintosh and HyperCard. No one was doing color for HyperCard so I took it apon myself to put something together. At about the same time Stan Gilbert came out with TKShowPict. His method was quite a bit better than my own so I stole it. What made his idea so important was NOT how to get at picture on the screen. That part was easy, but how to tell if it had been erased. While I will cover this in the background scripts and the script of the card button on the next card I did want to make sure credit was given where credit was due. Explore the scripts to get a good feel on how to fool folks into thinking HC really does color. Why not use his XCMD? Well Stan Gilberts was what I needed initally it had a two flaws: 1) I could not define a rectangle in which to place the picture. 2) I could not read in a PICT file. I needed to do both. So in the great tradition of necessity birthing invention. I took my ideas and merged them with his idea and came up with my own XCMD. Synatax ShowPicture [/], [/,[/],[Use Picture Rect ] Looks like ShowPicture 'Logo','0,0' Draw the PICT resource at card location 0,0. ShowPicture 'Bogus HD:Some Folder:My Picture', rect of cd btn 3, file Draw PICT file 'My Picture' at 'Bogus HD:Some Folder' into the rect of cd btn 3. ShowPicture 'Logo', rect of btn 2, True Draws PICT resource Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, File, True and ShowPicture 'MyHD:Logo', rect of bg btn 2, True, File Draws PICT File Logo at the upper right hand corner of btn 2 and returns the PICT size in the result. Software and Hardware Reqirements HyperCard version 2.0 and system 4.0 or greater. If there is great enough outcry I can re-rev it so that it will work with HC 1.2.x. Justification The ShowPicture XCMD places a great deal of the burden on HyperTalk programmer to 'know' the state of the HC window at any given time. Is this fair? I am not sure. It works fine for me and a few other people I know who have used this XCMD. It is very fast, the code is only 2000 bytes. Most of the code is used for parsing the XCMD arguments (and as such most is skipped). The only other way to keep track of when HC redraws it's window is to patch a trap or two. This has been done by the BungDabba folks. And if you want to worry less about when HC redraws over your color pictures their product, which is free, may interest you. If you want blazing speed this will do it for you. Even though it will look kinda tacky sometimes.

candidate.coreUserJob: Display an image asset inside a defined area of an application window and keep it visually in sync when the interface redraws.
candidate.whyRelevant: This maps cleanly to a durable module job: positioned media rendering with explicit layout bounds, file/resource loading, and redraw-state handling.
candidate.likelyStructure: command-style render API with image source, target rectangle, optional sizing return, and redraw/update coordination
candidate.searchQuerySeed: ui image rendering component fixed rectangle redraw state management

candidate.title: Down, A Novel
candidate.description: DOWN is fantasy/magic realism. The story opens in the ancient city of Levinfield, home of a civilization so old that their libraries collapse beneath the weight of their books, where the monetary system is based on poetry and . History follows a regular cycle, from industrial peak to decadent trough and back again, and the city is now in the middle of a quiet era of contented peace. Few people leave the city, as all their needs are catered for by the leftovers from the last industrial age, the automated Factories which can manufacture anything required and the antique mines where fossilized machines are dug from beneath the ruins. Lang, however, has been reading. In the libraries he uncovers references to a great forest to the West, and a desire stirs in him to explore, leave the safe streets and venture out into the deserted countryside. One legend in particular catches his interest. In the last days of the previous Industrial Age a truckdriver disappeared while passing through the forest on his way to the outlying villas of the rich Factory-owners. Since then his ghost was said to haunt the road, ceaselessly searching for a way out, a pale wraith in white tee-shirt and navy shorts, wandering through the trees crying "Map! Maaap!", searching for directions. Lang vows to track the ghost down and lay it to rest. His friends try and prevent him leaving. Subconsciously they fear he is the first sign of the next aggressive wave of greed, desire and development. But he is determined. He readies his rusted trailbike, acquires a longbow for protection, and sets out. Deep in the heart of the forest, in a castle that for millennia lay buried beneath the mold, Catherine toils obsessively at her life's work. She is a descendant of the Factory-owners of old, run-away from her parents crumbling villa to the West of the forest. She records on tape the words spoken by parrots that nest in the trees - words of a forgotten language of great power. Passed down through generations of birds, this language, (when the words are spoken in the right order), can harness strong magical energy. Catherine records and catalogues and files the words of power and never really knows why. As Lang unknowingly approaches her castle he encounters strange things. By night he hears the ghostly cry, "Maap!", and sees a white shape moving through the fog, until one night when the air is clear and he sees the ghost for what it really is - an albino deer, it's coat sparkling with phosphorescent spores, that bleats the mournful cry as it grazes. He decides to hunt it down and carry the skin back to the city to dispel the myth. Catherine, her senses amplified by magic, senses the deer's death. She knew it well, it was almost her pet. In her anger she decides to punish Lang in the worst way possible. She will create a void, a bottomless hole, an empty universe, and cast him in. For eternity he will float through space, alive, aware but unable to escape, lost in nothingness. As Lang casually heads back to Levinfield, the white pelt lashed across his pack, she works to prepare the spell, cutting and splicing tape in a mad frenzy. The trap works. One moment Lang is walking through the forest, the next he finds himself suspended in the highest room in the castle, an inky black well of nothing beneath his feet, Catherine standing before him triumphant. And then...? Read on, to find out how they both come to be trapped in the void, how together they escape from the impossible, how they come to find themselves in inner-city Sydney, in a world they have created from their dreams, a world corrupted by their antagonism and twisted by their desperation, a world which, finally, they have to decide either to allow to continue or to destroy utterly.

candidate.title: HyperKeys (Music & Sound)
candidate.description: Freeware version 1.0 HyperKeys Keyboard Music Writer allows you to write songs in hyperTalk format and save them to documents to be listened to or edited later. The Music Documents included with HyperKeys must be stored in the SAME folder as the HyperCard application itself. If they are not stored in that folder, then you will not be able to load the Music document (version 2.0 of HyperKeys will allow you to save to and open the documents from any location. These changes will be made to version 2.0, and all registered users get one free upgraded version). Using TeachText or HyperKeys Music Analyzer / Reader* (To be released following version 2.0) you can open the music documents and copy the song to be pasted into a HyperCard script (if you are a HyperCard Scipter). *HyperKeys Music Analyzer/Reader will be available at 25% off to anyone who has purchased from High Friction the HyperKeys version 2.0 software. candidate.title: Warp Speed Calculator v1.1 .sit
candidate.description: ----------------- Version 1.1 has been updated to calculate either for the original Enterprise (NCC-1701) or the newest Enterprise (NCC-1701-D) using UltraDrive Warp engines. Select the Star Ship; put in the distance (select either Light-years or Parsecs); then click on the desired warp speed. The travel time will then be computed and displayed. Have you ever wondered if Captain Kirk or Captain Picard knows what they are talking about when they quote a certain distance at a certain speed can be covered in a set amount of time????? Example: 'Number One, that's 23 light-years away! Why, that will take 3.5 days at Warp 9!' Well, wonder no more! With the Warp Speed stack you can compute the actual time to cross a given distance at a given speed using the official warp speed factors given in 'The Making of Start Trek' for the old Enterprise (NCC-1701) or the NEW warp speed factors for the new Enterprise (NCC-1701-D). This stack is freeware. Feel free to copy and distribute this stack for non-commercial purposes.

It is a very unusual place, in the midst of Hindu caste slavery, they promote inter-caste marriages. Gandhi offered to perform one there and was a friend of Gora, the founder. Gandhi was interested because the Atheist Centre was founded on social work and was renound in South India. He wondered how atheism could lead to good social work. Gora argued to Gandhi that atheism, not religion was universal, and that it was the only hope to get beyond the Moslem and Hindu fighting. This is a very interesting and unusual stack with music and some pictures. ----------------------REVIEW------------------------------- Very interesting and unusual! I don't think I can go to India now, but I would like to and would like to see the place in the stack. Never knew anything like this existed. --DanP4

candidate.title: capIt XFCN No installer.sit
candidate.description: ----------------- 'capIt' is an external function ('XFCN') for HyperCard or SuperCard that converts the first character or all characters in a word to upper- or lowercase. The word can be of any length, and in the form of any valid HyperTalk or SuperTalk container. A larger version of this stack that includes resources and scripts that will install capIt in another stack has also been uploaded to AOL. To install capIt from this version of the stack, you will nedd ResCopy or similar HyperCard resource utility.

candidate.title: CMU Tunnels
candidate.description: Newsgroups: alt.college.tunnels Subject: CMU Tunnels Hypercard stack (long) Date: 14 March 1994 Folks, I found something lying around on one of my old Mac disks that may interest some of you: an interactive hypercard map of the CMU steam tunnels. I wrote this stack back in '88 as part of a student application fair called StraightTalk. It's very simple, basically a map with hot spots that take you to pages describing the entrance or tunnel you clicked on. It was intended to demonstrate how you could do neat things with a Mac in a short period of time. It's not up to date, but I thought I would post it just for fun. Those of you who haven't actually been in tunnels can get a sense of how they are sometimes laid out, and I thought ex-CMU people might get kick out of reminiscing about old times. For current CMU people, you'll note the conspicuous absence of Resnik Hall & West Wing, the Parking Garage, and anything else that happened after 1988. Don't take this stack as an authoritative reference, but as a peek into the mid/late 80's CMU culture. The StraightTalk fair was officially sponsored, and no, I didn't get into any trouble for presenting this stack. I thought I would, and held my breath when a couple of Security folks came over, but nothing happened. Apple even had a photographer take pictures of all the presenters, including some of me near a tunnel entrance! I never saw the pictures, though... :( Anyway, here's the stack (called "Tunnels"). Remove everything above the "this file must be converted with binhex 4.0" line and then run it through binhex. Feel free to pass this on to other folks, put it on servers, ftp sites, etc. If you do, please send me a note letting me know so I can have a feel for its distribution. Ideally, I'd like it if the text portion of this message was included as a readme file so new folks would know some of the history. Enjoy, Dennis
candidate.modernAnalog: interactive facility or campus infrastructure map with clickable locations and detail pages
candidate.coreUserJob: browse a spatial layout and click points of interest to read location-specific information
candidate.whyRelevant: The durable pattern is a hotspot-based map that links places to structured detail views. That shape still fits modern campus, facility, utility, and site-orientation modules, though this example is niche and explicitly non-authoritative.
candidate.likelyStructure: overview map with clickable hotspots leading to per-location detail cards or pages
candidate.searchQuerySeed: interactive facility map clickable locations detail pages

candidate.title: Film Script
candidate.description: This stack is for all those budding screenwriters with a Mac. I developed this stack to get all those itty-bitty thoughts onto paper, you know, the ones that just don't fit anywhere? Anyway, Film script has several cards that should be pretty self-explanatory, but do try all buttons for added tips. Also, I don't recommend using command-recent to leave the stack. You'll get an annoying little script problem. Just ignore it. I plan to fix that in the next version. I also plan to make the 'note buttons'moveable so that you can organize your 'notes' in the order you like. Film Script is shareware--if you use it, it'll cost you just $20 to get the newer version, plus a discount toward TV Scripts. Yes, I'm also working on a TV script version which differs greatly from the Film script version as far as format goes. I'm always interested in your comments and suggestions. Please e-mail me: orbie.

candidate.description: Hi folks - This upload includes four original children's stories, presented in both English and Spanish in HyperCard stacks. I originally wrote these stories for my elementary school students. Some of the stories are whimsical and fun. Last month I uploaded the stories to the Macintosh Education Forum library with a suggestion that these stories might be nicely presented in HyperCard or HyperStudio format. Richard Wanderman, a talented HyperCard using educator from Connecticut, did a lovely job of creating these freeware stacks. Sofia Medina, a teacher from Sarasota, Florida, jumped in to assist with some of the translations. Hope these stacks prove useful to other teachers and parents. If there's anyone out there who can assist in some further translation work, I have a few other children's stories I'd like to share as freeware. It's really wonderful when educators are able to collaborate in this way. The fruits of our joint work can be enjoyed by all of our own students, as well as everyone else's students, too. The next challenge is to create some freeware quiz stacks which can quiz students on both the English and Spanish words used in these stories. Any volunteers to assist in creating the quiz stacks? Cheers, - Phil Shapiro Washington DC Internet: pshapiro@aol.com Oh, by the way, also enclosed in this archive are screen shots from these stack, imported into MacWrite II. These can be printed out quite easily from MacWrite II, ClarisWorks, or Microsoft Word. Note: The screen shots print better on laser printers than on inkjet printers. I like having a few copies of the screen shots printed out to show friends and colleagues who are not Mac users.
candidate.modernAnalog: bilingual reading-and-vocabulary lesson module for teachers and parents
candidate.coreUserJob: present children's stories in two languages and support follow-up vocabulary practice for learners
candidate.whyRelevant: This maps cleanly to a durable educational workflow: deliver parallel bilingual reading content, reuse it across classrooms and homes, and extend it with lightweight comprehension or vocabulary quizzes. The structure is modular and reusable for modern lesson cards, language-learning activities, and printable take-home materials.
candidate.likelyStructure: story set with parallel English/Spanish pages plus optional vocabulary quiz and printable handout views
candidate.searchQuerySeed: bilingual children's reading lesson vocabulary practice module

candidate.title: Astrology Treasure Hunt Demo
candidate.description: ASTROLOGY, PLANETS, SIGNS, HOROSCOPE, ZODIAC,TREASURE HUNT GAME, ASTEROIDS, FREEWARE DEMO, MAC ______________________ When is the Leo time to play, the Saturn time to work, the Venus time to love? Just as the Sun rises and sets every day, so do all of the other planets (Sun, Moon through Pluto, and asteroids Ceres, Pallas, Vesta, and Juno) have a 5-10 minute period when they are on the angles (rising, setting, culminating and at the nadir). This program generates a listing of when the planets are on the angles and when the angles change sign FOR YOUR LOCATION AND TIME. Includes interpretation for each planet and sign. I call the first, lowest level of significance, the 'forced' level. For example, if you see that Mars is on the Ascendant, you should look around for sharp or metal objects. You would know what you wanted to find (things, events, and people 'ruled' by Mars), and start looking for them. The next level I call the 'awareness' level. Keeping with the Mars example, you might become aware that you have been sitting on wrought iron chairs for an hour. The last, third, and highest level is the 'event' level. You might see on TV one or two police with all of their Mars equipment (gun, handcuffs, baton, etc.). This demo version has all the features of the commercial version except printing is limited (complete listing displayed on screen). The commercial version may be ordered from the author below for $25. The features new to 'Astrology Treasure Hunt 2.0c' are: => Asteroids Ceres, Pallas, Juno, Vesta and planet Chiron have been added. These symbolize many feminine traits not covered by Moon and Venus: Sexuality leading to spiritual awakening; nuturing all life; the rules necessary for one-to-one relationships; wisdom needed for people to live together cooperatively => Much expanded interpretation of all planets and signs => True type 'Glyphs' font print beautifully -- thanks to Time Cycles Research cooperation in my font development. => Retrogrades shown by 'R' Gerald Baron M.S Professional Astrologer since 1972 POB 426501 San Francisco, CA 94142-6501 (415) 751-4661 voice/eve & weekend Internet: GeraldCA@aol.com 'The years teach things the days never knew.' Checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with virex 5.6

candidate.title: The Bee
candidate.description: A bee randomly flies around in a room with a personâs face in it. If the bee lands on the personâs nose it will sting them and their eyes will roll around (like little marbles). If the bee hits any solid wall 10 times it will turn upside down and gently glide to the bottom, dead. If the bee flies through one of the four openings in each room, the little window will animate so that it looks like the room is shifting to the side to display another room with another, different person. This room movement will go on forever with the bee buzzing and bumping into walls (making a bump noise) until the bee dies or the user gets tired of this stupid thing and holds down a key to stop it. Iâm sorry, I canât remember the key at the moment but experiment and comment when you fine it. Note: there are a few easer eggs in The Bee: certain faces have âwormholesâ through their ears and if the bee enters one of them it will disappear, seemingly banging around inside their head, then emerge, either out of their other ear, or, in a special case, the window will shift and the bee will come out another personâs ear in another room. Enjoy. For more on the history of The Bee: https://richardsnotes.org/2005/03/24/the-bee/

candidate.title: The Manhole (HyperCard)
candidate.description: Like Alice in Wonderland , you are a child jumping into a magical world filled with fantastical places and peculiar characters. The world can be navigated screen by screen, by clicking the mouse where you want to go. Many objects on each screen can be clicked to find hidden animations and sounds. Exploring the completely open world, you can listen to and have educational conversations with Mr. Rabbit, Mr. Dragon, and others. The Manhole is aimed at children, and focuses on exploration and experimentation. There is no progress to be made, no goal to be reached, and the player can not fail. The entire world is accessible from the start. The original Macintosh version consists of six black-and-white stacks for the Macintosh's HyperCard software. Ports to other systems added color graphics. Also, the Macintosh floppy and CD versions are the same except for the cd version adding audio enhancements like voice-overs for the dialogue. This enhancement should not be confused with the remake of this game titled The Manhole: CD-ROM Masterpiece Edition . - ( MobyGames )

rawCard.title: Uncle Buddy's Phantom Funhouse
rawCard.description: Art "Buddy" Newkirk has disappeared and left you his literary estate. By the looks of it, he and his friends were a very odd bunch. You might have enjoyed knowing them. But you don't: why does "Uncle" Buddy think you do? Where is he, anyway? And what does this have to do with Meister Eckhart and the New York City subway? To find out, you'll have to pop the floppies into your Mac, drop the tapes into your boombox, and get ready to meet Buddy's friends, read his email, listen to his band, and sort out his (very strange) Tarot deck. Surreal and humorous, this is a world you will often return to and long remember.

candidate.title: Caper in the Castro
candidate.description: Artist Statement, 2023: "I regret the spelling errors found in the game. I am mildly dyslexic and suck at spelling. Back in 1988, I had no one to edit my writing. I also regret some of the more "cringe-worthy" humor that is inappropriate by today's standards. In some older video reviews as well as interviews, people have used the pronouns "she and her" when describing me. I am transgender and my preferred pronouns are "They, Them, and Their." Note: By default, the sound is off on this emulation. When the desktop boots up, click on the Apple icon in the upper left, select CONTROL PANEL, then click on SOUND and bring the volume level from 0 to 9. This will enable sound during the game. Programmed by CM Ralph (https://cmralph.com/) in 1989, Caper in the Castro is likely the first LGBT game. It was distributed via BBS, and is 'Charityware', with the designer asking players to donate to the AIDS Charity of their choice. You are the world famous lesbian private detective, Tracker McDyke. You are searching for a kidnapped drag queen, Tessy LaFemme. What you didn't count on was stumbling onto an even larger and more treacherous crime. The game was changed to different locations and a heterosexual them released under the name "Murder on Main Street". This version is located here . For more information about "Caper in the Castro": https://www.npr.org/2023/01/27/1151702216/how-the-first-lgbtq-video-game-was-given-a-second-life A Q&A With The Designer of the First LGBT Computer Game , by Luke Winkie, Paste Magazine, June 9, 2014. Caper in the Castro in the LGBTQ Video Game Archive. --------The following is taken from the original description / info text file:-------------- About "Caper in the Castro" This game is a Gay and Lesbian based adventure mystery featuring graphics, text, animation and sound. Written with HyperCard® Version 1.2. (Earlier versions will not be compatible) It is the first Gay and Lesbian computer game to be published in the world, as far as we know. In the game you assume the role of a lesbian private detective, Tracker McDyke, in search of a kidnapped drag queen, Tessy LaFemme. What you stumble onto is an even bigger and more hideous crime with a mystery that surrounds it. (Who is Dullagan Straightman? Who is the mysterious "L.L." that Straightman works for? What is the meaning of the cryptograph?) The game has a built-in help/tutorial if you get stuck, the best advice is to try everything and anything during your adventure. You never know what might happen! The game is packed with inside humor, puns and lots of perplexing problems. About the Author C.M. Ralph is a free lance artist living in the San Francisco Bay area. She supports her indulgence in art and computer game developing by working as a Quality Control Technician. Her work as an artist has spanned some 20 years and includes such notables as the design of the AIDS Response Program logo for Southern California's Orange County chapter, along with much donated work for many other nonprofit Gay and Lesbian Organizations. This game, as she describes it is "CharityWare" follows in this tradition of works for the Gay and Lesbian community. It is given out freely, with her request that if you enjoy playing it, please make a donation to an AIDS Related charity of your choice for whatever amount you feel is appropriate. The Inside Poop Caper in the Castro had a creation time of 6 months, It began in HyperCard version 1.0 and then was upgraded during construction. There was an additional 3 months of test playing in it's beta version. The final game version 1.0 was released in May of1989. Thank You's and credits appear when you click on the "About Game" button at the opening of the game. However there are some add-on's to be mentioned here: Thanks go to Diane, the sysop of Alternatives BBS (415) 922-5489 , for putting me in touch with Bill, the sysop of Fog City BBS (415) 863-9697 , (Who was enthusiastic enough to carry this game, thus allowing it nation-wide distribution.) Thank you Bill.

candidate.title: The Ultimate Warrior
candidate.description: The Ultimate Warrior is a HyperCard Dungeon Crawler Game developped by some college student (at that time) named Erick Ritchie, apparently. It portrays a simple yet addictive RPG where most of the gamplay is about clicking on arrows and pushing the enter key to progress the text (though you can also play the game entirely by mouse clicking, it's just less tedious using enter). I actually have no clue what the story is about, as I have a natural tendency to just spam the enter key to skip through the introduction. I played this game for the first time in 1996, and it stayed on my mind rent free ever since, so it shouldn't be too bad for a teenager project. A full playthrough of the demo is actually quite simple (yeah, unfortunately this is just a demo, and my efforts to contact that Erick guy were in vain, as it seems like the mail adress he left in the files to provide him feedback in 95' doesn't work anymore 30 years later, for some reason). Select a class for your character. Either Babarian, Ninja or Paladin. The two last classes have the advantage to let you cast spells, such as Fireball for the Ninja. Smash that enter key to skip through the story. Go to the temple, say hi, and skip the text. The temple is where you heal and save (note that saving quits the game). Go to the shop, and buy the first weapon useable for your class. Equip that weapon (the name of the weapon must be shown in the character's stats on the left of the screen, in the bottom, or it's not actively equipped). Go back to the town and head to the tunnels. Indeed, you can go to the tavern, but aside from a guy that tells you to go to the tunnels, and the bartender offering you free beverages to get you drunk (which doesn't work though, your character is too much of a badass unfortunately), there isn't anything to do. If you decided to buy a weapon an have it equiped, skip to 7. If you forgot to equip it or just skipped 4, perish on your first monster. You shouldn't have gone bare handed. You get ressurected with 1 HP. Go to the temple and heal before going back. Travel through the dungeon and have fun. The game is heavily based on RNG, and any hit, either from the character or a mob can range anywhere from 1 to 200 damage. In other words, while the game is not hard, it is very easy to die miserably on a weak enemy, even with the best gear, just because of bad luck. Just avoid getting there bare handed, and you will mostly be fine. The demo dungeon only provides two levels, which will roughtly take you around 2 hours, and when you beat the boss at the end of the second level, all the monsters just stop spawning. You just got your hands on a pretty great treasure and potions, but all of this is useless, since you won't encounter a single monster in this playthrough anymore. Why? Good question. I told you I spent my time skipping the story so, I don't know. The only thing you can do now is either start another playthrough with another class, or delete the demo. I would've adored a full game out of this, but I've yet to find the Erick guy. I'll keep you posted.
candidate.modernAnalog: A lightweight class-based dungeon crawler or interactive RPG flow with hub, inventory, healing, saving, and encounter progression.
candidate.coreUserJob: Pick a character class, prepare in town, explore a dungeon, survive encounters, and progress through a short RPG loop.
candidate.whyRelevant: The card implies a durable game-flow pattern: class choice, hub services, equipment gating, save/heal checkpoints, traversal, combat, boss completion, and replay with another class. That is reusable, but it is niche and game-specific rather than a broadly useful modern workflow module.
candidate.likelyStructure: class selection -> intro text -> town hub -> temple/shop/tavern actions -> equip gear -> dungeon traversal -> RNG combat -> heal/save recovery loop -> boss end state -> replay with different class
candidate.searchQuerySeed: class-based dungeon crawler hub shop heal save combat progression

candidate.title: Blah Blob!
candidate.description: Blah Blob! is a short platformer game made in HyperCard. You control the titular Blah Blob as you bounce through twenty levels of increasingly devious obstacles. Play the whole game straight through trying your best not to explode, or speedrun the levels individually or even the whole game and try to beat your best time. Perhaps even have some fun while doing so! (Please note the Blah Blob developer disavows all responsibility for excessive fun or frustration experienced as a direct result of or in conjunction with playing the game.) A Bit About The Backstory of Blah Blob What is HyperCard, you ask? Why it's an old and beloved app (or "application" to use 1980's Macintosh lingo) that made it fun and easy to create your own software. It was developed by Apple Computers for early Macintosh computers and originally released in 1987, and there are still many who fondly remember it to this day. The author of Blah Blob learned HyperCard as a child because "he wanted to make his own Super Mario" and gradually learned how to push it to its very limits. Now, thirty years later, he has returned to HyperCard to reconnect with those earlier days, have some fun making a game again, and seeing what he can manage to get it to do now that he's not constrained by such trifling obstacles as "slow processor speeds" or "software that wasn't meant to do this sort of thing". Blah Blob is more than just an anachronism. There is no point at which it made sense to release it. It could have been made back in 1990 when HyperCard 2.0 was released, but on any Macintosh at the time it would have run at an unplayable speed of around 2 frames per second. In 1999 it could run great on a PowerMac with a G4 processor, but by that point most would have considered it too late to make a game with purely monochrome graphics — HyperCard never had full support for color — on computers that routinely displayed millions of colors, or contemporaneous with game consoles that had full 3D graphics. Perhaps it makes the most sense that it was made and released in 2023, roughly twenty five years after HyperCard sadly lost its relevance in the world, at a time when some might appreciate its nostalgic qualities. Modern macOS, Windows, and Linux builds of the game are available here: https://bribrikendall.itch.io/blah-blob

candidate.title: The Macintosh Fanzine (German)
candidate.description: The Macintosh Fanzine is a contemporary and still active German Macintosh fanzine distributed as a HyperCard stack that is released roughly once every 3–4 months. The fanzine puts a focus on the family of Macintosh LC computers and is intended to be read on old Macs, hence the 800k images ready to be copied onto a real floppy. The stacks are of high quality, featuring neat b&w graphics and (sometimes) even sounds. Often a "goodie" like a freeware game or a save game is included—just like in the old days! (except this one got newly published in 2019!) All of the magazine is produced on authentic early 90s Macintosh hardware (some is written about this in various issues).

candidate.title: Cosmic Osmo (floppy version)
candidate.description: Cosmic Osmo is a point-and-click graphical adventure game for kids made by Cyan which would later make the hugely successful Myst series. candidate.title: The Manhole CD-ROM (1989 Activision release)
candidate.description: This is the original CD-ROM release of the Manhole (black and white version), published by Activision in 1989. It is identical to the 1988 floppy disk release except for the addition of a musical score, more sound effects and full audio voiceovers for all dialogue. The CD has an HFS data partition with the entire game (including the soundtrack and voice audio) contained inside multiple HyperCard stacks. The game therefore runs entirely from the data track of the CD. Also included is the full musical score for the game in 16 CD audio tracks which are playable through a sound system or computer-based CD audio player. See also: The Manhole CD-ROM Masterpiece Edition

candidate.title: Flowers of Crystal
candidate.description: Flowers of Crystal is an educational game from the author of Granny's Garden. The game was designed to provide for creative activities in the classroom and is built around puzzle-solving. It consists of three parts: an introductory story and an adventure in two parts. A password from the first part is required to start the second. The introductory story is also included recorded on an audio tape as well as printed in a booklet. The game takes place on the planet Crystal which once had vast expanses of colourful flowers that are now thought to be extinct. The player is a visitor from Earth who explores the planet in an attempt to find the last remaining flower. In the first part, the player has to find four objects (special water, gold pot, fertiliser and soil). The gameplay involves travelling between seven locations (centre, city, factory, forest, lake, desert and mountains) and choosing which items to carry. The game starts out at the centre where the player chooses four items from three different menus. Items are needed when visiting some of the locations or give some protection when encountering hazards or some of the world's hostile creatures. The second part involves finding six parts of a flower dream to recreate a complete flower picture and then go to a castle. To progress it is necessary to obtain spells and use them at the right location.
candidate.modernAnalog: classroom quest-based learning module with staged progression, inventory choices, and unlock codes between activities
candidate.coreUserJob: guide learners through a structured, story-driven problem-solving activity that breaks a lesson into sequential tasks and gated stages
candidate.whyRelevant: The durable value is not the fantasy setting or media bundle but the reusable pattern of classroom puzzle flow: intro context, multi-step scavenger/problem tasks, stateful item collection, and a gated handoff into a second phase. That maps to modern learning activities and workshop modules, though the specific adventure-game framing is niche.
candidate.likelyStructure: narrative introduction, phase-1 item collection across locations with constrained inventory, password-gated transition, phase-2 artifact assembly and location-specific actions
candidate.searchQuerySeed: classroom quest-based learning activity multi-stage puzzle progression unlock code

candidate.title: Cosmic Osmo and the Worlds Beyond the Mackerel (CD version)
candidate.description: Cosmic Osmo is a point-and-click graphical adventure game for kids made by Cyan which would later make the hugely successful Myst series. This is an open-ended exploring game where you can click on various elements on screen to trigger events, and clicking on doors or the edge of the screen will move you to another location. You first enter an animated spaceship which later is used to travel between different planets. Shortcuts also exist to other places in small spots like a mouse hole or a water drain. There is no way to die or get hurt in the game and you can't lose either. It was made originally on Macintosh using Hypercard, a combined software and run-time environment that mixes database capabilities with a graphical, flexible, user-modifiable interface. The game was one of the first on CD-Rom but a floppy disk version with fewer planets and no background music was also available. - ( MobyGames ) See also: Floppy version
candidate.modernAnalog: child-friendly interactive exploration module
candidate.coreUserJob: let a user safely explore a linked interactive world by clicking through scenes and discovering reactions
candidate.whyRelevant: The durable pattern is a non-linear, no-fail exploratory interface built from linked screens with clickable hotspots and lightweight navigation shortcuts. candidate.title: K'Kai Adventure
candidate.description: K'kai Adventure is a game in the form of a Hypercard stack, credited to a class of students at the Kaunakakai School in Hawai'i. The game is a simple point-and-click adventure set off the Hawai'ian coast, beginning with a simple crab dive and leading to exploring a mysterious underwater castle full of treasures and perils. The game is short and has cute scenes and a silly sense of humor, with branching paths leading to interesting bad ends, usually in the form of a cruel and unusual death. The short length makes exploring and finding all of them manageable, though, so even finding every room and way to perish won't take too long.
